Week of 1/20 - 1/23 (26)

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,915.61 (-0.65%)
    • SPX closes lower after a choppy week. News of Greenland tariffs, economic data, and major rotations into precious metals sent treasury yields spiking and the dollar tumbling.
  • NASDAQ Composite: 23,501.24 (-0.59%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 49,098.71 (0.74%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,940.22 (-0.11%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Moderna Inc (MRNA): 48.80 (17.24%)
      • Moderna shares rose after releasing data on their five year Phase 2b showing its personalized melanoma vaccine significantly reduced recurrence or death, boosting confidence in its oncology pipeline.
    • Micron Technology Inc (MU): 399.65 (13.18%)
      • Shares of MU rose this week after bullish reports cited that memory chip prices will continue to rise in the near term. With expectations to set quarterly record revenue, this increase in prices was not factored in. Both Stifel and TD keep a buy rating with TD upping PT to $450 a share.
    • Applovin (APP): 524.41 (-14.68%)
      • Shares fall as alleged claims that the ad company was used to launder money tied to Chinese Ponzi schemes and Southeast Asian fraud rings. The report claims major shareholder Hao Tang funneled billions in illicit funds through the company, and that AppLovin's software enabled illegal gambling apps to be installed on devices without user consent. While earnings are set for February 11th, APP is projecting revenue growth of 16% to $1.6 billion, and FY EPS estimates up over 100% to $9.32 per share.
    • Intel Corp (INTC): 45.08 (-8.48%)
      • Intel, the chipmaker who is up more than 100% over the past year, plunged 17% on Friday after reporting weak Q126 guidance in their Q425 report. Q4 revenue beat at $13.37B (-4.2% YoY) vs the $13.37B expected, and Adjusted EPS was up $.07 to $0.15. Despite these beats, Q126 revenue fell to $11.7-$12.7B and $0.00 EPS, vs Street expectations of ~$12.5B revenue and $0.05 EPS. Management also warned it can’t meet full demand due to manufacturing yield issues, while meaningful revenue from their new 14A foundry customers may not arrive until 2028.
        • Intel’s concerns over memory shortages and CPU shortages contributed to a larger pullback in the broader tech sector. Concerns over CPUs should actually be bullish for large chip manufacturing companies, where supply concerns lead to pricing power and more market share.

Bullish: AMD, AVGO, AMKR, ASX

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 4,136.16 (1.11%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 26,749.51 (0.41%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 53,846.87 (0.86%)

European Equities

  • UK Index (UKX): 10,143.44 (-0.90%)
  • German Index (DAX): 24,900.71 (-0.16%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 4,983.10 (8.22%)
    • Gold goes for $5,000 a troy ounce; Goldman Sachs raised price targets to $5,400 an ounce for the year end.
  • Silver Futures: 103.26 (13.83%)
    • Silver tops $100 as investors continue to pile into precious metals as concerns over Greenland helped push gold and silver up earlier in the week. Now, concerns over treasuries being dumped across the world leads investors to lean into “safer” assets as central banks move away from the USD.
  • Crude Oil Futures: 61.28 (3.92%)

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 87,863.51 (-0.52%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 2,886.39 (-1.70%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

TACO Trade: The US backed off their threats for Greenland tariffs after President Trump said he reached a framework deal with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte. Denmark and Greenland have both made clear that their sovereignty is non‑negotiable. Markets rallied after this news as the S&P 500 gained and gold and silver took small dips.

Donald “JP Morgan” Trump: President Trump sued JPMorgan Chase and CEO Jamie Dimon claiming the bank closed his accounts for political reasons after January 6, 2021. The lawsuit alleges JPMorgan acted on beliefs to hurt Trump and his family. JPMorgan says the suit "has no merit." The lawsuit dropped hours after Dimon publicly called Trump's proposed 10% credit card rate cap an economic disaster, warning it would cut off credit for roughly 80% of Americans.

Global Macroeconomic News

US GDP & Jobless Claims: US GDP for Q3 comes in at 4.4%, above expectations of 4.3%, while jobless claims come in at 200K vs 210K expected. Data shows that the economy is growing faster than expected while people are filing for unemployment less than expected. Probabilities for a January rate cut near 2%, but remain elevated for the earlier spring and late summer months. As long as unemployment remains stable and the economy continues to grow while earnings beat expectations, a rate cut may not be needed to continue into a bullish 2026.

*US Financing: Selling of US treasuries have sparked concerns over higher yields and a weakening USD ($97.46, -1.95% last 5 days). With foreign countries holding more than 40% of UST, warnings of excessive selling of such assets in response to US policy decisions could potentially drive borrowing costs up and hurt US equities. India’s holdings of UST have dropped to a five year low of $174B, now down 23% from its 2023 peak. India continues to try and strengthen the Rupee while relying less on foreign reserves. The EU also holds 10T in US assets alone.

In other related news, Japan has also seen heavy rotations out of its national reserves, with long dated bonds reaching record high yields. As the largest holder of US treasury bonds, this could serve as a warning that US finances are insecure. Citadel's Ken Griffin remarked that if US treasuries begin to be viewed as risky, bonds and stocks will move together in price, leading to heightened yields and implications for mortgages and US debt financing.

Japan Outlook & JPY: BoJ Governor Kazuo Ueda held rates steady at 0.75% and reaffirmed Japan's rate hike path for the near future. Signaling a cautious approach despite increased inflation forecasts, his comments helped nudge the Yen slightly lower before the largest one-day surge since August. The Yen reached a record of 155.69 per dollar while the USD had its biggest ever drop against the Japanese currency. Traders reported that the Federal Reserve Bank of New York had called in a rate check, indicating that both US and Japanese officials may be ready to intervene in the Asian currency market. Concerns over government borrowing continue as Japan’s snap election is set to take place on February 8th.

Chip News: In chip related news China has authorized tech firms to place orders for Nvidia’s H200 chips, letting them negotiate quantities while still promoting the use of domestic alternatives. CEO Jensen Huang also remarked this week that the global AI buildout will still require trillions of dollars in investments. At a 5.4T equity value, NVDA is still trading at over a 24 forward PE.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones

Noah Halfon
